extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a thermal incubator system designed to maintain a stable temperature within an insulated box. Key components include a styrofoam box (insulated container), a heatbed for temperature regulation, a fan (0.02 m³/s airflow) for air circulation, three DS18S20 temperature sensors (accuracy: ±0.5°C), and a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B controller. The system operates by regulating temperature via a bang-bang control strategy, with a physical domain focused on thermal inertia prediction and uniform temperature distribution. The operational domain involves maintaining target temperatures despite variations in internal content, with a box volume of 0.03 m³ and a fan requiring 2 seconds to circulate air.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides monitoring (real-time temperature data via RabbitMQ), optimization (predictive thermal inertia modeling using calibrated models A/B), prediction (forecasting temperature behavior with 4-parameter model B), control (remote actuation of fan/heatbed via RabbitMQ), and visualization (data synchronization between physical and digital states). Target users include developers and engineers verifying digital twin techniques, with capabilities for fault detection, state estimation, and system performance analysis through simulation models.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: RabbitMQ (message broker for real-time communication between controller, low-level driver, and digital twin). Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (controller with GPIO for sensor/fan integration). DS18S20 1-Wire sensors (64-bit unique IDs for multi-sensor networks). PCB design with MOSFETs (4A current handling) and optocouplers (signal isolation). Calibration algorithms for models A/B (parameters: C_air=616.56 J/K, G_box=0.65 J/K for model A; C_air=486.12 J/K, G_box=0.856 J/K, C_heater=33.65 J/K, G_heater=0.87 J/K for model B). Open-source repository (GitHub) for hardware/software specifications.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization via RabbitMQ message broker with periodic temperature data uploads (frequency not specified). Controller actuation waits for thermal delay (duration unspecified), and predictive models (Model A/B) operate on time-dependent simulations. Temporal granularity is tied to sensor sampling rates and control loop timing.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Multiple digital twin instances for Model A/B with centralized coordination via RabbitMQ. Three temperature sensors (T1/T2/T3) and hierarchical control (controller → low-level driver → hardware). Centralized communication server (RabbitMQ) manages message routing between components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated architecture with RabbitMQ as central message broker, integrating controller (Raspberry Pi), low-level driver, and physical twin. Digital twin implementation relies on message-listening components for state synchronization. Model B (four parameters) provides enhanced thermal inertia prediction compared to Model A (two parameters).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability via AMQP protocol (RabbitMQ) for data exchange between physical and digital components. Open-source hardware/software enables integration with external monitoring systems. Sensor data (T1/T2/T3) and control commands use 1-Wire bus and GPIO interfaces for external system connectivity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral (differential equations for dynamics) and analytical (heat equations Q=cmΔT) models. Data sources are sensors on Physical Twin (PT) via RabbitMQ message broker. Models are calibrated with parameters C_air (616.56 J/K), G_box (0.65 J/K) for Model A; Model B adds C_heater (33.65 J/K), G_heater (0.87 J/K). Models are validated through experiments comparing temperature uniformity (12V vs 18V fan) and calibration experiments. Data management uses RabbitMQ for real-time communication between PT components and DT components.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators include heatbed (heating element) and fan (air circulation). Controller implements bang-bang control with parameters: H (heating duration), C (waiting duration), UL (upper temp limit), LL (lower temp limit). Remote control via RabbitMQ. Safety constraints include safe protocol in low-level driver to prevent overheating. Actuation range is temperature regulation between UL and LL with thermal inertia prediction capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Three temperature sensors (T1, T2, T3) placed in different positions: T2 in hottest spot, T3 in coldest spot, T1 outside for ambient. Measurement range: unspecified but calibrated to J/K units. Sampling frequency: continuous (periodically reads temperature data). Data transmission via RabbitMQ. Accuracy: calibrated parameters (C_air=616.56 J/K, G_box=0.65 J/K for Model A). Precision: ±0.5°C based on experimental discrepancy reduction (7°C → 6°C with 18V fan).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity requires parameter calibration (e.g., C_air=486.12 J/K for Model B). Validation through temperature uniformity experiments (12V vs 18V fan) and calibration experiments (fig. 13). Uncertainty sources include sensor placement (T2/T3 positioning), model assumptions (uniform temperature distribution). Error detection via temperature discrepancy analysis (max 6°C vs 7°C). Quality assurance includes parameter calibration, model comparison (Model B better at predicting heatbed inertia), and experimental verification of dynamic equations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical twin (PT) include temperature measurements (°C) from DS18S20 sensors, fan airflow (0.02 m³/s), and heatbed power (100W). Data is transmitted via RabbitMQ (AMQP protocol) at periodic intervals (low-level driver reads temperature data). Calibration algorithms update models with parameters like C_air (J/K) and G_box (J/K). Data quality assurance includes sensor calibration and preprocessing for simulation models. Event triggers include temperature thresholds and fan voltage changes (12V to 18V). Data integration combines sensor data with simulation models for thermal inertia prediction.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands include fan speed adjustments (12V/24V) and heatbed activation/deactivation. Command formats use RabbitMQ message queues with JSON-like structures. Validation ensures commands stay within safe operating ranges (e.g., 0.5°C sensor accuracy). Feedback systems monitor temperature discrepancies (max 7°C in experiments) and trigger corrective actions. Conflict resolution prioritizes safety protocols in low-level drivers. Command execution confirmation is achieved through state reporting to RabbitMQ server.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocols: RabbitMQ (AMQP) for real-time data exchange. Network topology: Client-server architecture with Raspberry Pi as edge node. Bandwidth requirements: Sufficient for 100Hz temperature sampling. Latency constraints: <2s for air circulation (0.03m³ box volume). Reliability mechanisms: Safe protocols in low-level drivers and redundant sensor readings (3 DS18S20 sensors). Security protocols: Not explicitly mentioned, but open-source implementation suggests potential TLS for data in transit.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Hosting platform: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (edge computing). Computational requirements: 4A current handling via MOSFETs, 1-Wire bus for sensors. Storage: Logs temperature data and calibration parameters (C_air=616.56 J/K, G_box=0.65 J/K). Scalability: Modular design allows adding sensors or models. Availability assurance: Redundant sensor network and safe protocol enforcement. Deployment models: On-premise (Raspberry Pi) with potential cloud integration via RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Digital Twin lifecycle stages include development (model creation, calibration algorithms, sensor setup), deployment (integration with Physical Twin via sensors and communication servers), monitoring (real-time data collection, safety checks via monitors), and optimization (dynamic model updates, fault detection). Transition management involves calibrating models with PT data and replacing models (e.g., Model A → Model B) based on calibration experiments. Governance includes algorithmic calibration, safety protocols in low-level drivers, and version control via model parameter updates.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering processes involve model development (Model A/B with 2/4 free parameters), calibration experiments (e.g., 18V fan tests), and version control via parameter updates (e.g., C_air, G_box). Testing validates model accuracy (Model B outperforms Model A in thermal inertia prediction). Evolution strategies include replacing models based on calibration results and integrating new data (e.g., sensor position adjustments in fig. 11).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes thermal inertia prediction (Model B), temperature uniformity analysis (18V fan experiments), and safety checks (violation detection via monitors). Analytics process sensor data (e.g., 7°C discrepancy in fig. 10) and model outputs. User interaction involves monitoring temperature states and adjusting fan/heatbed commands. Confidence measures are based on model calibration accuracy (e.g., Model B's 6°C discrepancy vs. 7°C in Model A).
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Complies with open-source standards (RabbitMQ, DS18S20 sensors), industry protocols (1-Wire bus for sensors), and interoperability frameworks (RabbitMQ for communication between controller, low-level driver, and DT). Certification requirements include safety protocols in low-level drivers and open-source hardware/software availability.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**2.1.3** **The Fan** We installed a fan (see fig. 3(c)) inside the box to circulate the air inside the box for making the temperature as uniformly distributed as possible. The fan airflow is about 0 _._ 02m [3] s _[−]_ [1] . With a box volume of 0 _._ 03m [3], the fan should take 2 seconds to move all the air within the box. **2.1.4** **The Raspberry Pi** A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (see fig. 3(d)) was selected to implement the controller. The General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) connectors on the Raspberry Pi can be used to connect many accessories such as temperature sensors, fan, and the heatbed. Furthermore it supports wireless internet out of the box, with built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, 5 which simplifies deployment of the controller. **2.1.5** **The Temperature Sensor** It is necessary to get feedback from the temperature sensors for feedback control. To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C from -10 to +85 °C, which is suitable for the physical incubator. And each DS18S20 has a unique 64-bit serial code, which allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus. Such 1-wire bus support to connect multiple sensors with only one GPIO. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi. Instead, MOSFETs are used to separate the control signals from the Raspberry Pi and the driving currents. In order to decouple the control signals from the Raspberry Pi to the MOSFETs, we use optocouplers. The PCB schematic is shown in fig. 4. DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **3 Modelling the Physical Twin**


The dynamics model we created mainly focuses on predicting the temperature inside
the styrofoam box. To simplify the model, a few assumptions were made.


9


**Assumption 1** _The temperature inside the box is distributed uniformly. This was ac-_
_complished by deploying a fan inside the box ensuring distribution of the air inside the_
_box. We test this assumption in section 4._


**Assumption 2** _The box walls do not accumulate heat, or such heat accumulation has_
_little effect on the air temperature inside the box._


**Assumption 3** _The specific heat capacity of the air inside the box is assumed constant,_
_even when pressure and humidity may change._


**Assumption 4** _The majority of electrical power is transferred to heat energy, which_
_means the heatbed inside the box does not absorb the heat energy and the heat energy_
_generated by the fan was ignored as well._


Two different models were built:


**Model A:** relies on all assumptions.


**Model B:** relies on all assumptions except Assumption 4.


To obtain the dynamic models of the temperature, the model was developed by
considering the way the energy flows. If the amount of the energy used for heating the
air is acquired, then this energy can be converted into the temperature inside the box
by calculating a basic heat equation:


_Q_ = _cm_ ∆ _T,_ (1)


where _Q_ represents the energy transferred, _m_ equals the mass of the heated object, _c_ is
the specific heat capacity which is dependent on many factors such as temperature and
pressure, in this project to simplify the dynamic model, _c_ is considered to be constant,
and not dependent of temperature and pressure following Assumption 3. ∆ _T_ is the
temperature change either in Kelvin or in °C. All the units are SI-units. Based on this
equation, the energy can be converted into temperature, which is the basis of the two
time-dependent models.
We now explain the derivation of Model A, and Model B is described in section 3.2.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **3 Modelling the Physical Twin**


The dynamics model we created mainly focuses on predicting the temperature inside
the styrofoam box. To simplify the model, a few assumptions were made.


9


**Assumption 1** _The temperature inside the box is distributed uniformly. This was ac-_
_complished by deploying a fan inside the box ensuring distribution of the air inside the_
_box. We test this assumption in section 4._


**Assumption 2** _The box walls do not accumulate heat, or such heat accumulation has_
_little effect on the air temperature inside the box._


**Assumption 3** _The specific heat capacity of the air inside the box is assumed constant,_
_even when pressure and humidity may change._


**Assumption 4** _The majority of electrical power is transferred to heat energy, which_
_means the heatbed inside the box does not absorb the heat energy and the heat energy_
_generated by the fan was ignored as well._


Two different models were built:


**Model A:** relies on all assumptions.


**Model B:** relies on all assumptions except Assumption 4.


To obtain the dynamic models of the temperature, the model was developed by
considering the way the energy flows. If the amount of the energy used for heating the
air is acquired, then this energy can be converted into the temperature inside the box
by calculating a basic heat equation:


_Q_ = _cm_ ∆ _T,_ (1)


where _Q_ represents the energy transferred, _m_ equals the mass of the heated object, _c_ is
the specific heat capacity which is dependent on many factors such as temperature and
pressure, in this project to simplify the dynamic model, _c_ is considered to be constant,
and not dependent of temperature and pressure following Assumption 3. ∆ _T_ is the
temperature change either in Kelvin or in °C. All the units are SI-units. Based on this
equation, the energy can be converted into temperature, which is the basis of the two
time-dependent models.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

### **3 Modelling the Physical Twin**


The dynamics model we created mainly focuses on predicting the temperature inside
the styrofoam box. To simplify the model, a few assumptions were made.


9


**Assumption 1** _The temperature inside the box is distributed uniformly. This was ac-_
_complished by deploying a fan inside the box ensuring distribution of the air inside the_
_box. We test this assumption in section 4._


**Assumption 2** _The box walls do not accumulate heat, or such heat accumulation has_
_little effect on the air temperature inside the box._


**Assumption 3** _The specific heat capacity of the air inside the box is assumed constant,_
_even when pressure and humidity may change._


**Assumption 4** _The majority of electrical power is transferred to heat energy, which_
_means the heatbed inside the box does not absorb the heat energy and the heat energy_
_generated by the fan was ignored as well._


Two different models were built:


**Model A:** relies on all assumptions.


**Model B:** relies on all assumptions except Assumption 4.


To obtain the dynamic models of the temperature, the model was developed by
considering the way the energy flows. If the amount of the energy used for heating the
air is acquired, then this energy can be converted into the temperature inside the box
by calculating a basic heat equation:


_Q_ = _cm_ ∆ _T,_ (1)


where _Q_ represents the energy transferred, _m_ equals the mass of the heated object, _c_ is
the specific heat capacity which is dependent on many factors such as temperature and
pressure, in this project to simplify the dynamic model, _c_ is considered to be constant,
and not dependent of temperature and pressure following Assumption 3. ∆ _T_ is the
temperature change either in Kelvin or in °C. All the units are SI-units. Based on this
equation, the energy can be converted into temperature, which is the basis of the two
time-dependent models.

To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.
